title: The Silent Choke & Surgical Crisis
subtitle: Obstructive sleep apnea and sleep surgery
description: OSA screening, PSG severity grading, CPAP compliance, DISE-guided surgery, postoperative hemorrhage, and discharge wound care.

steps.title: Step 1/6: Clinical Symptoms and Screening
steps.scenario: A 36-year-old man with BMI 26 visits the ENT clinic because of severe long-term snoring. His family has witnessed apneic episodes during sleep.
steps.media: Load image: SNOT-22, ESS, and STOP-Bang questionnaires
steps.question: To quickly screen for high-risk undiagnosed OSA in clinic and help identify moderate to severe disease, which questionnaire should you use?
steps.feedback:
Correct answer: C

Attending tip: STOP-Bang includes snoring, tiredness, observed apnea, hypertension, BMI, age, neck circumference, and gender. It is a practical clinical screening tool for undiagnosed OSA and can help identify patients with moderate to severe disease.

steps.title: Step 2/6: PSG and Severity
steps.scenario: To confirm the diagnosis, you arrange overnight polysomnography. The report shows an apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) of 33.7 events per hour.
steps.media: Load image: PSG tracing showing airflow cessation with oxygen desaturation
steps.question: Based on this AHI value, what severity grade is this patient's obstructive sleep apnea?
steps.feedback:
Correct answer: C

Clinical definition: AHI less than 5 is normal, 5-15 is mild OSA, 15-30 is moderate OSA, and AHI of 30 or higher is severe OSA. This patient's AHI is 33.7, which is severe and, with daytime sleepiness, requires active treatment.

steps.title: Step 3/6: Medical Management and Compliance
steps.scenario: For severe OSA, you first recommend the noninvasive gold-standard treatment: nasal CPAP. One month later, the patient returns and says he cannot sleep with the mask because leak and claustrophobia are intolerable. He strongly requests surgery. Physical examination shows bilateral grade II tonsillar hypertrophy and Friedman class 2 tongue position.
steps.question: What is the current medical definition of CPAP compliance?

options.text: (A) Use on more than 5 nights per week and more than 4 hours per night.
(B) The device must be worn for more than 8 hours every day, whether daytime or nighttime.
(C) Wearing it for more than 30 minutes before sleep counts as compliant.

steps.feedback:
Correct answer: A

Clinical pitfall: CPAP compliance is generally defined as use on more than 5 nights per week and more than 4 hours per night. This young patient cannot tolerate CPAP because of mask discomfort, which is one of the most common reasons to consider surgical intervention.

steps.title: Step 4/6: DISE and Surgical Intervention
steps.scenario: Because the patient chooses surgery and OSA is often caused by multilevel obstruction, you arrange drug-induced sleep endoscopy (DISE) under anesthesia to observe the dynamic sites of collapse. You then perform uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP) and transoral robotic surgery for tongue-base reduction.
steps.media: Load image: DISE view showing soft-palate and tongue-base collapse during sleep
steps.question: Why is DISE a key step in modern sleep surgery before UPPP and TORS?

options.text: (A) To directly remove the tonsils in clinic.

(B) Awake physical examination cannot fully reflect collapse during sleep after muscle relaxation; DISE dynamically identifies obstruction sites such as the soft palate or tongue base and guides surgical planning.
(C) To perform biopsy and exclude nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

steps.feedback:
Correct answer: B

Procedure interpretation: In patients with severe AHI plus tonsillar hypertrophy and tongue-base issues, obstruction is often three-dimensional. DISE simulates sleep and lets the surgeon see whether collapse is from the soft palate, lateral pharyngeal walls, tongue base, or multiple levels, guiding whether TORS tongue-base reduction is needed.

steps.title: Step 5/6: Postoperative Complication: Delayed Hemorrhage
steps.scenario: The operation is completed smoothly and the patient has been discharged. On postoperative day 13, he suddenly rushes to the emergency department because of massive oropharyngeal bleeding and epistaxis. His vital signs are stable, but fresh blood keeps pouring into the oral cavity. Endoscopy shows a massive blood clot over the tongue base, making the bleeding point unclear.
steps.media: Load image: Emergency endoscopic view showing a massive clot over the tongue base and tonsillar fossa
steps.question: For delayed bleeding after UPPP and TORS, what should first-line emergency conservative management and evaluation include?

options.text: (A) Blindly clamp deep in the tongue base in the emergency department.
(B) Secure the airway, check coagulation labs, apply local epinephrine by inhalation or compression, and give intravenous tranexamic acid.

steps.feedback:
Correct answer: B

Emergency nightmare: Delayed postoperative bleeding after TORS and UPPP is one of the most frightening complications, especially when clot accumulates at the tongue base and threatens suffocation. In this case, the emergency team excluded wound dehiscence, used epinephrine spray and tranexamic acid to stabilize the bleeding, and admitted the patient for supportive care.

steps.title: Step 6/6: Discharge Instructions and Wound Care
steps.scenario: After hemostatic medication and short-term nasogastric tube placement, the wound recovers well with no further bleeding. The patient tolerates cold liquid diet and is ready for discharge.
steps.media: Load image: Postoperative cold liquid diet instruction sheet
steps.question: Which postoperative care principle should you strongly emphasize at discharge?

options.text: (A) Resume hot soup and hard food immediately after discharge to regain strength.
(B) The wound may still ooze during the first 2 postoperative weeks. Avoid forceful coughing, maintain a cold liquid diet during the first week, and hold ice water in the mouth if bleeding occurs.
(C) Sing loudly every day to rehabilitate the throat muscles.

steps.feedback:
Correct answer: B

Attending reminder: After oropharyngeal mucosal surgery, especially tonsil and tongue-base surgery, the pseudomembrane sloughing period around postoperative days 7-14 is the peak time for delayed bleeding. Forceful coughing must be avoided, and a strict cold liquid diet such as cold milk or pudding plus ice therapy helps constrict vessels and prevent life-threatening hemorrhage.
